ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for keema matar. FOODON_03311737 (meat curry food product) is the closest defensible parent class covering minced-meat curry preparations.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Meat based dishes) is the closest parent. No specific keema matar entry exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for keema matar or sufficiently specific minced-meat curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of keema matar is approximately 250 g, usually accompanied by naan bread or steamed basmati rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mutton Keema (minced mutton curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted for keema matar with green peas
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods provides values for mutton keema (minced mutton curry). Keema matar adds green peas (~15–20% by weight), which modestly increases carbohydrate and dietary fibre and slightly dilutes protein and fat per 100 g. Values adjusted proportionally. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (Lamb, ground, cooked, pan-browned; USDA NDB 17283) used to cross-validate protein and fat values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for keema matar in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat curry preparations. The dominant macronutrient is protein and fat; carbohydrate content is low (6.2 g/100 g), primarily from green peas (GI ~22–48, University of Sydney GI Database). The blended dish GI is estimated conservatively at 30. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 15.5 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.65, rounded to 4.7.

hypertension.guidance: Keema matar contains approximately 320 mg sodium per 100 g (800 mg per restaurant serving), which represents a significant portion of the 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target recommended for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 30 and only 6.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, keema matar has a low glycemic load (~4.7 per serving) and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from lamb mince
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Keema matar provides approximately 4.2 g saturated fat and 52 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and consider leaner mince alternatives, as saturated fat intake above 7% of total energy is associated with elevated LDL cholesterol.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keema Matar is a classic North Indian and Pakistani curry dish made from minced meat — traditionally lamb or mutton, though beef, chicken, or a vegetarian soy substitute may be used — cooked together with green peas (matar) in a richly spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by first browning finely minced onions with ginger and garlic, then adding whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and chilli, followed by the minced meat which is cooked until the fat separates, and finally green peas which are simmered until tender. The result is a semi-dry, intensely flavoured curry with a savoury, mildly spiced profile and a slightly grainy texture from the minced meat contrasted with the soft bite of the peas. Keema matar is a staple of Mughal-influenced North Indian cuisine and is widely served across the Indian subcontinent, in Indian diaspora communities globally, and in Indian restaurants in Singapore. Nutritionally, it is a high-protein, moderate-fat, low-carbohydrate dish. Per 100 g it provides approximately 165 kcal, 12.5 g protein, 10.8 g fat (of which 4.2 g is saturated), and 6.2 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is low (~30), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high (~320 mg/100 g) due to added salt and spice blends. It is a good source of iron (2.1 mg/100 g), zinc (3.2 mg/100 g), and vitamin B12 (1.4 mcg/100 g) from the lamb. Clinically, it is appropriate for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its low GI and low carbohydrate load, but sodium and saturated fat content warrant attention for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free in its traditional preparation.
common_user_queries: Is keema matar good for diabetics?
What meat is used in keema matar?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for mutton keema with proportional adjustment for green peas content, cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy lamb mince values. No exact keema matar entry exists in any approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate based on the dish's macronutrient profile and University of Sydney GI Database values for comparable meat curry and pea components. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (mutton keema) with proportional adjustment for approximately 15–18% green peas by cooked weight. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 17283, lamb ground cooked pan-browned) used for cross-validation of protein and fat. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish category. 5. GI of 30 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for keema matar was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is marked true as lamb/mutton keema is the traditional preparation and is halal when sourced from a halal-certified butcher; restaurant-specific halal certification should be verified independently. 7. Sodium value of 320 mg/100 g is an estimate based on typical restaurant-style Indian curry preparation; home-cooked versions may be lower. 8. The dish is served at two field survey data restaurants (Maharani Table and Kinara, both Boat Quay) at SGD 19.90–21.00.
